text.title: Solo Niche Narrative — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for refining the "I am the <X> for <Y> who needs <Z>" statement from paying-customer evidence.

rule.statement: Define <Y> only from customers who PAID, not those who replied / nodded / inquired. Cohort threshold: customers contributing ≥ 70% of trailing-quarter revenue.
rule.rationale: Positioning that follows attention rather than wallets converges on a brand that is interesting but unprofitable. The 70%-revenue rule names the actual buyer.

rule.statement: The <Z> (the need) field must use language spoken by ≥ 3 customers in the cohort. No marketing copy, no LLM rewrites, no "value proposition synonyms".
rule.rationale: The customer's verbalised problem is the only positioning anchor with predictive power. Marketing rewrites optimise for click-through, not recognition by future buyers.

rule.statement: Output must include 1-3 customer types EXCLUDED from <Y>, each with a one-line reason citing real lost-deal evidence. "Positioning for everyone" is rejected.
rule.rationale: Strong positioning is recognisable by who it repels. Without exclusions, positioning becomes a meaningless universal claim.

rule.statement: The new statement must change at least one of {X, Y, Z} compared to the previous quarter's statement. If all three are identical, the framework concludes "no refinement needed" and returns the prior statement with a stability marker — DO NOT produce cosmetic synonym changes.
rule.rationale: Operators rewrite positioning quarterly because the calendar says so, generating thesaurus-grade noise that confuses prospects. The drift gate forces a real signal-based change or no change.

rule.statement: If the operator wants <X> or <Y> to be a category they have NOT been paid for in the trailing quarter, this methodology REJECTS the change and routes to `problem-validation`. Niches are refined from evidence, not declared.

rationale.source: Trevor Owens — Lean Enterprise / Brennan Dunn

rule.rationale: Self-declared niche shifts ("I want to focus on AI startups now") without paying evidence drop quarterly revenue ~30% as the existing pipeline thinks the operator quit their old service.

example.title: Refined statement (passes all rules)
example.code:
"I am the API integration developer for D2C Shopify brands doing $1-10M GMV who need their Klaviyo and ERP to stop dropping order data on Black Friday."
X = API integration developer (concrete role, not "freelancer") Y = D2C Shopify brands at $1-10M GMV (cohort with 70% revenue evidence) Z = "stop dropping order data on BF" (verbatim quote from 4 customers)
example.note: Specific X, evidenced Y, verbatim Z. Excludes: enterprise (lost 2 deals on red-tape), pre-revenue (low budget).

example.title: Aspirational (r5 violation)
example.code:
"I am the AI agent architect for VC-backed AI startups who need to ship MVPs fast."
Operator's prior quarter had 0 AI-startup customers. Pure aspiration. Framework rejects → run problem-validation on the AI-startup niche first.
example.note: Illustrates r5 — methodology refuses to legitimise wishful niche shifts.
